title: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
author: Lewis Carroll
gist: Drowsy on a summer riverbank, Alice follows a hurried, waistcoat-wearing White Rabbit down its hole into a world that runs on dream-logic. Cakes and potions wrench her size; animals lecture her; a grinning cat evaporates; a tea-party never ends. The deeper she goes, the more the nonsense hardens into menace, until the Queen of Hearts' croquet-ground and courtroom demand her head. Grown too large — in every sense — to be ruled by playing cards, Alice scatters the dream and wakes with her head in her sister's lap.

segments.text: A bored afternoon splits open when a White Rabbit checks its pocket-watch and dives underground. Alice falls after it — slowly, past cupboards and shelves — into a hall of locked doors, where a golden key fits only a door too small to use. A bottle marked DRINK ME telescopes her down; a cake marked EAT ME sends her up nine feet; her own tears become a salt pool she must swim. Already the rules are clear: her body is negotiable, and every remedy overshoots. She paddles ashore in strange company, soaked and much changed.

On the bank the dripping animals try to get dry — first with the driest history lecture on record, then with the Dodo's Caucus-race, which everybody wins and Alice must furnish the prizes for. The Mouse's long tale trails off in offense, and talk of her cat Dinah empties the shore. Then the White Rabbit reappears and, mistaking Alice for his housemaid, sends her to his house, where another bottle swells her until she fills the building. Besieged and pelted with pebbles that turn to cakes, she shrinks, escapes an enormous puppy, and finds a tall mushroom — with something sitting on top of it.

A hookah-smoking Caterpillar interrogates her — 'Who are you?' — the one question she can no longer answer, having been so many sizes since morning. Its parting gift, a mushroom whose two sides push her up and down, finally gives her some control, though a Pigeon first condemns her telescoping neck as a serpent's. In the Duchess's pepper-choked kitchen a baby turns to a pig in her arms, and the Cheshire Cat maps the neighborhood: everyone here is mad. Proof waits at the March Hare's table, where it is always six o'clock, tea never ends, and riddles have no answers. She walks out — and through a little door, into the beautiful garden at last.

The garden belongs to the Queen of Hearts, whose gardeners paint white roses red to dodge the axe and whose every third sentence is 'Off with his head!' Croquet is played with flamingo mallets and hedgehog balls; the Cheshire Cat's disembodied grin defeats an executioner's logic. Between rounds Alice endures the Duchess's morals, then is marched off to hear the Mock Turtle sob through his sea-school education and demonstrate, with the Gryphon, the Lobster Quadrille. Her recitations keep coming out as someone else's words — until a distant cry announces that the trial is beginning.

The Knave of Hearts stands accused of stealing the tarts, in a courtroom where the King is judge, jurors write down their own names to remember them, and witnesses are threatened with execution for being nervous. Called to testify, Alice is growing again — too big for the room and the rules, so Rule Forty-two is invented to remove her. The evidence collapses into an unsigned poem about nobody. When the Queen demands sentence before verdict, Alice's contempt breaks the spell: the court is nothing but a pack of cards, their fluttering only dead leaves as she wakes on the bank with her head in her sister's lap.

segments.heading: I · Down the Rabbit-Hole
segments.text: Alice, bored beside her sister on the riverbank, sees nothing so very remarkable in a White Rabbit muttering to itself — until it takes a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket. Burning with curiosity she follows it down a rabbit-hole that becomes a well so deep she has time, falling, to lift a marmalade jar from a passing shelf and to wonder drowsily whether cats eat bats. She lands unhurt in a long hall lined with locked doors, where a little golden key opens only a fifteen-inch door onto the loveliest garden you ever saw. A bottle labelled DRINK ME shuts her up like a telescope — with the key left glittering on the glass table far overhead — and then a small cake says EAT ME, so she eats it.

segments.heading: II · The Pool of Tears
segments.text: The cake works with a vengeance: 'Curiouser and curiouser!' cries Alice, opening out like the largest telescope that ever was, so tall she plans to send Christmas presents to her own far-off feet. The White Rabbit scurries by and flees her giant voice, dropping his fan and white kid gloves — and leaving her a harder problem: if she can't remember her lessons and doesn't feel like herself, who exactly is she now? The fan shrinks her almost to nothing, and she slips into a pool of her own earlier tears. There she treads salt water beside an affronted Mouse — twice offended by talk of cats and dogs — and finally leads a bedraggled flotilla of birds ashore.

segments.heading: III · A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
segments.text: The soaked assembly's first problem is getting dry. The Mouse tries the driest thing it knows — a recitation of English history — before the Dodo proposes a Caucus-race: a rough circle, everyone starting and stopping when they like, and at the end everybody has won and all must have prizes. Alice supplies them from her pocket, one comfit apiece, and receives her own thimble back with full ceremony. Then the Mouse begins its long and sad tale, which Alice cannot help picturing as a long and winding tail, and her wandering attention drives it off in a huff. When she adds how ably her cat Dinah would fetch it back, the whole party recalls urgent appointments elsewhere.

segments.heading: IV · The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
segments.text: The White Rabbit returns hunting his fan and gloves and, taking Alice for his housemaid Mary Ann, orders her to his house — where she finds another little bottle and, drinking on principle, grows until one arm is out the window and one foot up the chimney. The siege that follows sends Pat the gardener and Bill the Lizard against her; Bill goes up the chimney and comes out of it like a skyrocket, propelled by Alice's foot. When the mob's pebbles turn to little cakes on the floor, she eats one, shrinks, and bolts into a wood, where she must dodge the affections of a puppy the size of a cart-horse before finding a tall mushroom — and on top of it, arms folded, a large blue caterpillar quietly smoking a hookah.

segments.heading: V · Advice from a Caterpillar
segments.text: The Caterpillar's first question is the one Alice cannot answer: 'Who are you?' Three inches of contemptuous calm, it makes her recite 'You are old, Father William' — which arrives wrong from beginning to end — and, crawling away at last, leaves the only useful answer of the interview: one side of the mushroom will make her grow taller, and the other side shorter. Trial and error slams her chin against her feet, then shoots her neck above the treetops, where an outraged Pigeon insists that any long-necked creature who eats eggs is a serpent, whatever it may call itself. Nibbling carefully from each hand, she works herself down to nine inches and sets out for a little house four feet high.

segments.heading: VI · Pig and Pepper
segments.text: A Fish-Footman delivers a Frog-Footman an invitation from the Queen: the Duchess is to play croquet. The Duchess's kitchen proves to be a storm of pepper and airborne saucepans, where the cook hurls crockery, the baby howls, and a large cat grins from ear to ear because — the Duchess explains — it is a Cheshire cat. Handed the baby to nurse, Alice carries it outside, where it becomes, unmistakably and rather to its improvement, a small pig, and trots off. From a bough overhead the Cheshire Cat appears and vanishes at leisure, supplying the local map: a Hatter one way, a March Hare the other, and everyone here mad — Alice included, or she wouldn't have come.

segments.heading: VII · A Mad Tea-Party
segments.text: At a great table set under a tree, the March Hare, the Hatter, and a sleeping Dormouse cry 'No room!' though there is plenty. The party runs on rules of its own: nonexistent wine, personal remarks, and a riddle with no answer — why is a raven like a writing-desk? The Hatter's watch tells the day of the month, two days wrong; he quarrelled with Time at the Queen's concert last March, and in revenge it is now always six o'clock, always tea-time, the party moving round the table as the cups are used up. The Dormouse's story of three sisters living in a treacle-well dissolves into rudeness, and Alice walks out — through a door in a tree, back into the long hall, and this time, key and mushroom managed correctly, into the beautiful garden.

segments.heading: VIII · The Queen’s Croquet-Ground
segments.text: In the garden, three gardeners — playing cards on legs — are hurriedly painting a white rose-tree red, having planted the wrong one, when the procession arrives. The Queen of Hearts' interview with Alice runs from 'What's your name, child?' to 'Off with her head!' inside a minute, and Alice hides the condemned gardeners in a flower-pot. The croquet that follows uses live flamingoes for mallets, hedgehogs for balls, and doubled-over soldiers for arches, everyone playing at once while the Queen sentences the field to execution. Overhead the Cheshire Cat materializes grin-first, and King, Queen, and executioner deadlock over whether a head with no body can be beheaded — until the head fades entirely, taking the case with it.

segments.heading: IX · The Mock Turtle’s Story
segments.text: The Duchess, unexpectedly amiable away from the pepper, attaches herself to Alice's arm, digs her sharp little chin into Alice's shoulder, and finds a moral in everything — until the Queen's shadow falls across them and she is gone in a moment. The Queen then marches Alice to a sleeping Gryphon and dispatches the pair to the Mock Turtle, who sits sobbing on a rock over sorrows that, the Gryphon confides, are entirely his own fancy. Once he was a real Turtle: he went to school in the sea under an old Tortoise ('we called him Tortoise because he taught us'), studying Reeling and Writhing, Uglification, and lessons that lessened from day to day — which is why they were called lessons.

segments.heading: X · The Lobster Quadrille
segments.text: Cheered by his own grief, the Mock Turtle joins the Gryphon in teaching Alice the Lobster Quadrille: two lines along the seashore, a lobster for a partner, advance twice, change lobsters, throw them as far out to sea as you can, and swim after them. They dance a figure round her to the whiting's song — 'Will you walk a little faster?' — and unpack the deep etymology of whiting, soles, and porpoises. Invited to tell her own history, Alice so unsettles the company that they demand a recitation instead, and ''Tis the voice of the sluggard' comes out as a Lobster, with a Panther in the second verse that was never there before. The Beautiful Soup chorus is cut off by a distant cry: 'The trial's beginning!'

segments.heading: XI · Who Stole the Tarts?
segments.text: In the courtroom the King of Hearts sits as judge with his crown balanced over his wig, the tarts sit in evidence on a table, and the twelve jurors busily write down their own names before they forget them. The accusation is a nursery rhyme: the Queen of Hearts, she made some tarts; the Knave of Hearts, he stole those tarts. The first witness is the Hatter, who arrives with teacup and bread-and-butter, testifies chiefly to the twinkling of the tea while guinea-pigs are suppressed head-first into canvas bags, and finally bolts for the door ahead of the Queen's afterthought about his head. The cook pleads pepper and vanishes; and as Alice feels herself quietly beginning to grow, the White Rabbit shrills the next witness's name: 'Alice!'

segments.heading: XII · Alice’s Evidence
segments.text: Leaping up, Alice tips the jury-box over with her skirt-hem and must shovel the jurors back like spilt goldfish. Her evidence — she knows nothing whatever about the business — strikes the King as very important, then, on correction, unimportant; and when Rule Forty-two (all persons more than a mile high to leave the court) is invented against her, she refuses to go. The prosecution falls back on a letter of unsigned verses that name nobody and prove, the King reasons, everything. 'Sentence first — verdict afterwards,' rules the Queen; 'Stuff and nonsense! You're nothing but a pack of cards!' rules Alice, at her full size now — and the pack rises, flutters down upon her, and turns to dead leaves as her sister wakes her from the strangest of dreams beside the river.

segments.heading: I · Down the Rabbit-Hole
segments.text: Alice is drowsing over her sister's pictureless book, weighing whether a daisy-chain is worth the trouble of getting up, when a white rabbit with pink eyes runs close by lamenting 'Oh dear! I shall be late!' — and takes a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket to check. She is on her feet and after it across the field, straight down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge. The hole becomes a well so deep, or her fall so slow, that she lifts an empty jar of ORANGE MARMALADE from a passing cupboard, muses on latitude, longitude, and the antipathies, and drifts toward a dream of walking hand in hand with her cat Dinah — until, thump, she lands on a heap of sticks and dry leaves, not a bit hurt.

She is up in a moment, just in time to see the White Rabbit turn a corner — 'Oh my ears and whiskers, how late it's getting!' — and then it is gone, and she stands alone in a long, low hall lit by lamps hanging from the roof. Every door is locked; but a little golden key on a three-legged glass table fits a fifteen-inch door behind a low curtain, and through it lies the loveliest garden you ever saw, beds of bright flowers and cool fountains — reachable by not so much as her head. On the table now stands a bottle ('which certainly was not here before') labelled DRINK ME; and having checked that it is not marked poison, Alice tastes it — cherry-tart, custard, pine-apple, roast turkey, toffee, hot buttered toast — and very soon finishes it off.

'What a curious feeling!' She is shutting up like a telescope: ten inches high, exactly the size for the little door — but the golden key still lies on the glass table, hopelessly overhead now, and the slippery table-legs defeat every attempt to climb. The small Alice sits down and cries, then lectures herself sharply for it: she is fond of pretending to be two people, though there is hardly enough of her left at present to make one respectable person. Presently her eye falls on a little glass box under the table, holding a cake with EAT ME beautifully spelled out in currants. Whichever way this one works, it must change something; so she sets to work, and very soon finishes the cake.

segments.heading: II · The Pool of Tears
segments.text: 'Curiouser and curiouser!' cries Alice — so surprised she forgets her good English — as she opens out like the largest telescope that ever was. Her feet recede almost out of sight, and she plans, absurdly, to keep on their good side by post: 'Alice's Right Foot, Esq., Hearthrug, near the Fender (with Alice's love).' Then her head strikes the roof of the hall: nine feet high, she snatches the little golden key and hurries to the garden door — where all she can do is lie on one side and look through with a single eye. Sitting down, she cries gallons, until a large pool spreads halfway down the hall, and scolds herself all the while: a great girl like her, to go on crying in this way!

A pattering of feet: the White Rabbit returns, splendidly dressed, fan in one hand and white kid gloves in the other — and bolts in terror at Alice's giant, timid 'If you please, sir—', dropping both. Fanning herself with his fan, she works at the day's great question: if she is not herself, who is she? Not Ada, whose hair goes in ringlets; surely not Mabel, who knows so very little — yet four times five keeps making twelve, London has become the capital of Paris, and 'How doth the little crocodile' comes out praising a beast that welcomes little fishes in with gently smiling jaws. Mid-crisis she finds she has pulled on one of the Rabbit's tiny gloves: the fan is shrinking her, fast. She drops it just in time — and then her foot slips, and she is up to her chin in salt water.

She has fallen into the pool of her own tears, wept when she was nine feet high — 'I wish I hadn't cried so much!' Something splashes nearby: a Mouse, paddling. Her opening gambit — 'Où est ma chatte?', the first sentence in her French lesson-book — makes the poor creature leap half out of the water, and every apology digs her in deeper: Dinah is such a dear quiet thing, and such a capital one for catching mice — oh, I beg your pardon! — and the neighbors' terrier, so handy for killing rats — oh dear! The Mouse, quivering to the end of its tail, twice threatens to swim off, then relents: it will tell her its history on shore, and explain why it hates cats and dogs. By now the pool is quite crowded — a Duck and a Dodo, a Lory and an Eaglet, and several other curious creatures — and the whole party swims for the bank.

segments.heading: III · A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale
segments.text: A queer-looking, dripping party assembles on the bank — draggled feathers, fur clinging close, everybody cross — and the first question is how to get dry. The Mouse, evidently a person of authority, tries the driest thing it knows: 'William the Conqueror, whose cause was favoured by the pope, was soon submitted to by the English...' — delivered with an important 'Ahem!' and shivered through to no effect whatever. So the Dodo rises solemnly to move that the meeting adjourn for the immediate adoption of more energetic remedies — 'Speak English!' cries the Eaglet, to general applause — namely, a Caucus-race. And the best way to explain a Caucus-race, says the Dodo, is to do it.

The course is marked out in a sort of circle — the exact shape doesn't matter — and everybody starts where they like and leaves off when they like. After half an hour's running, when all are quite dry again, the Dodo suddenly calls 'The race is over!' and, after long thought with one finger pressed to its forehead, delivers judgment: everybody has won, and all must have prizes. Alice, appointed to give them, gravely hands round comfits from her pocket, exactly one apiece; her own prize is her own thimble, presented back to her by the Dodo with the words 'We beg your acceptance of this elegant thimble.' The comfits are eaten with much noise and confusion — the large birds complaining they cannot taste theirs, the small ones choking — and then the circle re-forms to hear the Mouse's story.

'Mine is a long and a sad tale!' sighs the Mouse. 'It is a long tail, certainly,' says Alice, looking down with wonder — and as the Mouse speaks, the story winds through her mind in the very shape of a tail: Fury and the mouse, a trial with no judge or jury, cunning old Fury to take both parts and condemn the whole to death. Caught not attending — 'You are not attending!' — and then fatally punning on knots, Alice gives offense past repair, and the Mouse stalks away despite the whole party calling after it. Worse follows: wishing aloud for Dinah, who would soon fetch it back, Alice is asked who Dinah is, and her glowing portrait of the bird-catching cat empties the bank — the Magpie remembering that night air disagrees with its throat, the Canary gathering its children — until she is all alone again, and near crying.

segments.heading: IV · The Rabbit Sends in a Little Bill
segments.text: The White Rabbit trots back, anxiously hunting the lost fan and gloves, and on catching sight of Alice orders her home in the voice one uses to a housemaid: 'Why, Mary Ann, what are you doing out here? Run home this moment and fetch me a pair of gloves and a fan!' Too startled to explain, she runs to a neat little house with W. RABBIT engraved on a brass plate, finds the fan and gloves upstairs — and beside them, inevitably, a little bottle, this one with no label at all. She drinks on principle ('I know something interesting is sure to happen'), and before half is gone her head is pressing the ceiling: she must kneel, then lie down with one elbow against the door and one arm out of the window, wondering what can possibly have become of her — and whether, having filled the house, she has at least stopped growing older.

The Rabbit arrives demanding his gloves, finds his own front door blocked by Alice's elbow, and lays siege. His gardener Pat is consulted about the object in the window — 'Sure, it's an arm, yer honour!' 'An arm, you goose! Who ever saw one that size? It fills the whole window!' — and a snatch at it produces two shrieks and a crash of broken cucumber-frames. Next, Bill the Lizard is sent down the chimney; Alice's sharp upward kick returns him — 'There goes Bill!' — like a skyrocket, to be revived on brandy by the crowd. The Rabbit's threat to burn the house down is met with 'If you do, I'll set Dinah at you!'; then a barrowful of pebbles rattles through the window, and the pebbles turn, on the floor, into little cakes. She eats one, shrinks delightedly, and runs out past the whole assembly — poor Bill propped up and drinking — into a thick wood.

Her plan now has two points: get back to her right size, and find her way into that lovely garden. The wood supplies a complication first — an enormous puppy, round-eyed and delighted, stretching one paw down at her. To a creature Alice's size its friendliness is a mortal hazard: she offers it a stick and dodges behind a great thistle through the whole game of fetch, the puppy charging and tumbling head over heels, until it sits down panting at a distance, tongue hanging out. Escaped — and reflecting what a dear little puppy it would have made, if only she were the right size to teach it tricks — she casts about for something to eat or drink to grow on, and finds a large mushroom about her own height. Stretching on tiptoe to peer over the edge, she meets the eyes of a large blue caterpillar, arms folded, quietly smoking a long hookah and taking not the smallest notice of her.

segments.heading: V · Advice from a Caterpillar
segments.text: 'Who are you?' says the Caterpillar. It is not an encouraging opening, and Alice's reply — 'I—I hardly know, sir, just at present: I know who I was when I got up this morning, but I think I must have been changed several times since then' — does not improve matters. The interview proceeds by contradiction and hookah-smoke: changing sizes is not confusing, being three inches high is a very good height indeed (the Caterpillar is exactly three inches), and her temper wants keeping. Ordered to prove her memory, she recites 'You are old, Father William': the old gentleman stands incessantly on his head, back-somersaults in at the door, finishes the goose bones, beak and all, and balances an eel on the end of his nose. 'That is not said right,' says the Caterpillar. 'It is wrong from beginning to end.'

More smoke, more silence — and then, at last, the useful question: 'What size do you want to be?' Alice isn't particular as to size, only one doesn't like changing so often; a little larger than three inches would be nice ('It is a very good height indeed!' says the Caterpillar angrily, rearing itself upright). In a minute or two it takes the hookah out of its mouth, yawns, shakes itself, and crawls away into the grass, remarking merely, as it goes: 'One side will make you grow taller, and the other side will make you grow shorter.' One side of what? 'Of the mushroom,' says the Caterpillar, exactly as if she had asked aloud. Defeated by the geometry of a perfectly round mushroom, Alice at last stretches her arms round it as far as they will go and breaks off a bit of the edge with each hand. A nibble from the right hand slams her chin against her foot; frightened, she forces down a morsel from the left.

Now her shoulders are nowhere to be found: her neck rises like a stalk out of a sea of green leaves, and as she zigzags it down among the treetops a large Pigeon flies into her face, beating her with its wings and screaming 'Serpent!' The Pigeon has tried the roots of trees, and banks, and hedges, and there is no pleasing serpents; three weeks without a wink of sleep, and just as it takes the highest tree in the wood, down they come wriggling from the sky. A creature with a neck like that will not mend its case by claiming to be a little girl — especially one who admits, being truthful, that little girls eat eggs. 'If they eat eggs, they're a kind of serpent,' rules the Pigeon, and settles sulkily back onto its nest. Alice, crouching among the branches, nibbles first one bit and then the other, grows sometimes taller and sometimes shorter, and works herself down at last to nine inches — the right size, she judges, for the little house four feet high standing in the open place ahead.

segments.heading: VI · Pig and Pepper
segments.text: From the wood she watches a Fish-Footman deliver to a Frog-Footman a letter nearly as large as himself: 'For the Duchess. An invitation from the Queen to play croquet' — exchanged with bows so low their powdered curls entangle. The Frog-Footman then installs himself on the doorstep to explain, with maddening calm, why knocking is useless: he can only answer the door from inside, and the extraordinary noise going on within — a constant howling and sneezing, and every now and then a great crash as of a dish or kettle broken to pieces — would drown any knock in any case. He proposes to sit there, on and off, for days and days. The door opens just long enough for a large plate to skim out and graze his nose. 'There's no sort of use in talking to him,' says Alice desperately, 'he's perfectly idiotic!' — and she opens the door and walks in herself.

The door leads straight into a large kitchen full of smoke: the Duchess on a three-legged stool nursing a howling baby, the cook stirring a cauldron of soup with far too much pepper in it, and a large cat sitting on the hearth grinning from ear to ear. ('It's a Cheshire cat,' says the Duchess, 'and that's why. Pig!') The cook, meanwhile, throws everything within reach — fire-irons, saucepans, plates — at the Duchess and the baby, while the Duchess takes no notice even when hit, and offers philosophy instead: the world would go round a deal faster if everybody minded their own business; talking of axes, chop off her head. Her lullaby — 'Speak roughly to your little boy, and beat him when he sneezes' — comes with a chorus of 'Wow! wow! wow!', the cook and baby joining in. Then, flinging the baby at Alice ('You may nurse it a bit, if you like! I must go and get ready to play croquet with the Queen'), she is gone; and the little creature grunts, its nose turns up, its eyes grow small — until Alice sets down, with real relief, a small pig, which trots quietly off into the wood.

On the bough of a tree a few yards off sits the Cheshire Cat, grinning. It supplies directions with perfect logic: in that direction lives a Hatter, in that direction a March Hare — visit either you like, they're both mad. 'But I don't want to go among mad people.' 'Oh, you can't help that: we're all mad here. I'm mad. You're mad.' 'How do you know I'm mad?' 'You must be, or you wouldn't have come here.' Its proof of its own madness — a dog growls when it's angry and wags its tail when it's pleased, whereas the Cat growls when pleased and wags when angry — does not entirely convince her, but its manner of vanishing and reappearing (once to ask whether the baby became a pig or a fig) is beyond dispute; the last time it vanishes quite slowly, beginning with the end of the tail and ending with the grin, which remains some while after the rest of it has gone. A cat without a grin Alice has often seen; a grin without a cat is the most curious thing she ever saw in all her life. She walks on toward the March Hare's house, whose chimneys are shaped like ears and whose roof is thatched with fur.

segments.heading: VII · A Mad Tea-Party
segments.text: Before the house a table is set out under a tree, laid for a great many; the March Hare and the Hatter are having tea at one corner of it, using a fast-asleep Dormouse between them for a cushion, and all three cry 'No room! No room!' as Alice approaches. There is plenty of room, and she sits down in a large arm-chair. The hospitality proceeds accordingly: the March Hare offers wine, of which there is none ('Then it wasn't very civil of you to offer it.' 'It wasn't very civil of you to sit down without being invited'); the Hatter observes that her hair wants cutting; and his riddle — 'Why is a raven like a writing-desk?' — cheers her briefly, until the company establishes that saying what she means and meaning what she says are not the same thing a bit, any more than 'I see what I eat' is 'I eat what I see'. On that the conversation drops, and the party sits silent for a minute.

The Hatter breaks the silence by asking what day of the month it is, consulting a watch that tells the day but not the hour — and is two days wrong. 'It was the best butter,' the March Hare offers meekly, of what got into the works; crumbs must have gone in too. The riddle is given up: the Hatter hasn't the slightest idea of the answer, nor has the March Hare, and Alice's sigh — she thinks they might spend their time better than wasting it on riddles with no answers — brings out the truth. 'If you knew Time as well as I do,' says the Hatter, 'you wouldn't talk about wasting it. It's him.' On good terms with Time, you might have the clock do anything you liked — half-past one, dinner-time, in a twinkling. But the Hatter shakes his head mournfully: 'Not I! We quarrelled last March — just before he went mad, you know' (pointing his teaspoon at the March Hare).

The quarrel came at the great concert given by the Queen of Hearts, where the Hatter had to sing 'Twinkle, twinkle, little bat! How I wonder what you're at! Up above the world you fly, like a tea-tray in the sky' — and had hardly finished the first verse before the Queen bawled 'He's murdering the time! Off with his head!' Ever since, Time won't do a thing he asks: it is always six o'clock now, always tea-time, with no leisure even to wash the things between whiles, so they simply keep moving round the table as the cups are used up. ('But what happens when you come to the beginning again?' Alice ventures. 'Suppose we change the subject,' the March Hare interrupts, yawning.) A story is demanded; Alice doesn't know one; so 'Then the Dormouse shall!' — and the Dormouse, pinched awake from both sides, opens its eyes and begins, in a great hurry: 'Once upon a time there were three little sisters, and their names were Elsie, Lacie, and Tillie; and they lived at the bottom of a well—'

The sisters lived on treacle, being in a treacle-well ('They couldn't have done that, you know: they'd have been ill.' 'So they were — very ill'), and they were learning to draw, and drew all manner of things — everything that begins with an M: mouse-traps, and the moon, and memory, and muchness — did you ever see a drawing of a muchness? Alice's objections ('But they were in the well.' 'Of course they were: well in') and the Hatter's fresh rudenesses — a clean cup all round, the Hatter alone profiting by the move — wear her out at last: 'Then you shouldn't talk,' says the Hatter, and this is more than she can bear. She walks off in great disgust; the Dormouse is asleep before she is out of sight, and looking back she sees the other two trying to put it into the teapot. In the wood she notices a tree with a door leading right into it, and inside is the long hall again, close by the little glass table: and this time, wiser, she takes the golden key first, then nibbles the saved mushroom to a foot high, and walks down the little passage into the beautiful garden at last, among the bright flower-beds and the cool fountains.

segments.heading: VIII · The Queen’s Croquet-Ground
segments.text: A large rose-tree stands near the entrance of the garden: its roses are white, but three gardeners are at it, busily painting them red. Alice watches the curious sight — Five splashing paint on Seven, Seven flinging down his brush, Two beginning, in a low voice, the explanation: this ought to have been a red rose-tree, and a white one was put in by mistake, and if the Queen was to find it out, we should all have our heads cut off, you know. So here they are, doing their best, before she comes, to— At which point Five, who has been anxiously watching the garden, cries 'The Queen! The Queen!' and the three gardeners instantly throw themselves flat upon their faces.

The procession comes: ten soldiers carrying clubs, ten courtiers ornamented with diamonds, the royal children decorated with hearts, guests mostly Kings and Queens — among them the White Rabbit, talking fast and nervously — the Knave of Hearts carrying the King's crown on a crimson velvet cushion, and last of all, THE KING AND QUEEN OF HEARTS. The Queen's interview with Alice runs from 'What's your name, child?' through 'And who are these?' (the flattened gardeners, their card-backs indistinguishable) to 'Off with her head!' in under a minute; Alice's loud, decided 'Nonsense!' silences her, and the King murmurs that she is only a child. The gardeners are sentenced for their tulip-root mistake and tucked by Alice into a large flower-pot as the soldiers hunt them; the soldiers report the heads gone ('Their heads are gone, if it please your Majesty!'); and the Queen roars 'Can you play croquet?' — 'Yes!' shouts Alice — 'Come on, then!'

In the procession Alice finds herself walking beside the White Rabbit, who is peeping anxiously into every face and whispering. The news he risks — on tiptoe, mouth close to her ear, looking over his shoulder — is that the Duchess is under sentence of execution, for boxing the Queen's ears. Alice begins to laugh; 'Hush!' begs the Rabbit in a frightened tone, 'the Queen will hear you!' The story goes no further, for a voice of thunder shouts 'Get to your places!' and people begin running about in all directions, tumbling up against each other.

It is the most curious croquet-ground Alice has ever seen — all ridges and furrows; the balls live hedgehogs, the mallets live flamingoes, the arches soldiers doubled over on hands and feet. Her flamingo twists round to look up in her face whenever she means to strike; the hedgehog unrolls and crawls off; the players all play at once without waiting for turns, quarrelling and fighting for the hedgehogs; and the Queen stamps about shouting 'Off with his head!' or 'Off with her head!' about once in a minute. Then, in the air above her, appears a grin, then eyes, then the whole head of the Cheshire Cat, to whom Alice at last has someone to complain: they don't play at all fairly, and they all quarrel so dreadfully, and the Queen— (the Queen being close behind: '—is so likely to win, that it's hardly worth while finishing the game'). The King objects to the Cat's look; the executioner's argument is that you cannot cut off a head unless there is a body to cut it off from; the King's, that anything that has a head can be beheaded; the Queen's, that if something isn't done about it in less than no time, she'll have everybody executed all round. Alice's suggestion — it belongs to the Duchess, you'd better ask her — sends for the prisoner; but the Cat's head fades away while the executioner is gone, and by the time the Duchess arrives it has entirely disappeared.

segments.heading: IX · The Mock Turtle’s Story
segments.text: 'You can't think how glad I am to see you again, you dear old thing!' says the Duchess, tucking her arm affectionately into Alice's — amiable now, Alice thinks, because of the absence of pepper; maybe it's always pepper that makes people hot-tempered, and vinegar that makes them sour. The walk proceeds under the Duchess's uncomfortably sharp chin, dug into Alice's shoulder, and her mania for morals: ''Tis love, 'tis love, that makes the world go round'; 'Birds of a feather flock together'; 'Be what you would seem to be' — with a longer, more entangled version on offer. Flamingoes and mustard both bite; mustard is agreed to be a mineral, or a vegetable, or possibly the product of a large mustard-mine near here ('the moral of that is: the more there is of mine, the less there is of yours'). Then the Duchess's voice dies away in the middle of her favourite word 'moral': the Queen stands before them, arms folded, frowning like a thunderstorm, and offers a fine day and a choice — 'Either you or your head must be off, and that in about half no time!' The Duchess takes her choice, and is gone in a moment.

'Let's go on with the game,' says the Queen, and Alice, too frightened to say a word, follows her back to the croquet-ground. The game resumes under the royal eye; the Queen never leaves off quarrelling with the other players and shouting 'Off with his head!' or 'Off with her head!', the sentenced taken into custody by soldiers who must leave off being arches to do it — until, in half an hour or so, there are no arches left, and all the players except the King, the Queen, and Alice are in custody and under sentence of execution. Then the Queen leaves off, quite out of breath: 'Have you seen the Mock Turtle yet?' It is the thing Mock Turtle Soup is made from. They come upon a Gryphon, lying fast asleep in the sun — 'Up, lazy thing! Take this young lady to see the Mock Turtle, and to hear his history' — and as the Queen walks off, the Gryphon half laughs, half to itself, half to Alice: 'What fun! It's all her fancy, that: they never executes nobody, you know. Come on!'

They find the Mock Turtle sitting sad and lonely on a little ledge of rock, sighing as if his heart would break — sorrow that, the Gryphon repeats, is all his fancy. 'Once,' says the Mock Turtle at last, with a deep, hollow sob, 'I was a real Turtle.' The history follows, with long silences and occasional 'Hjckrrh!'s from the Gryphon: a school in the sea, kept by an old Turtle they called Tortoise — 'because he taught us, really you are very dull!' — with the best of educations: Reeling and Writhing to begin with, then the different branches of Arithmetic — Ambition, Distraction, Uglification, and Derision; Mystery, ancient and modern, with Seaography; and from the classical master, Drawling, Stretching, and Fainting in Coils. Lessons ran ten hours the first day, nine the next, and so on — 'that's the reason they're called lessons: because they lessen from day to day' — a scheme by which the eleventh day must have been a holiday, and about whose twelfth the Gryphon firmly changes the subject: 'That's enough about lessons. Tell her something about the games now.'

segments.heading: X · The Lobster Quadrille
segments.text: The Mock Turtle sighs deeply, draws the back of one flapper across his eyes, and — swallowing his sobs — describes, turn and turn about with the Gryphon, the Lobster Quadrille. You first form into two lines along the sea-shore: seals, turtles, salmon, and so on (the jellyfish cleared out of the way first — that generally takes some time); you advance twice, each with a lobster as a partner; change lobsters, and retire in same order; then you throw the lobsters as far out to sea as you can, swim after them, turn a somersault in the sea, change lobsters again, and back to land — and that is all the first figure. It must be a very pretty dance, says Alice timidly; would she like to see a little of it? Very much indeed. 'Come, let's try the first figure!' says the Mock Turtle to the Gryphon. 'We can do without lobsters, you know. Which shall sing?' 'Oh, you sing — I've forgotten the words.'

So they begin solemnly dancing round and round Alice, every now and then treading on her toes when they pass too close, and waving their forepaws to mark the time, while the Mock Turtle sings, very slowly and sadly, the whiting's song: 'Will you walk a little faster?' said a whiting to a snail. 'There's a porpoise close behind us, and he's treading on my tail!' — will you, won't you, will you, won't you, will you join the dance? The dance done (Alice: 'Thank you, it's a very interesting dance to watch — and I do so like that curious song about the whiting!'), the natural history follows. Whiting, it develops, do the boots and shoes under the sea — what are shoes done with, on land? Blacking, exactly: under the sea, whiting — and they are made of soles and eels, of course. And no wise fish would go anywhere without a porpoise: 'Don't you mean purpose?' asks Alice. 'I mean what I say,' the Mock Turtle replies, in an offended tone.

Invited to tell her own adventures, Alice begins from the morning — no use going back to yesterday, because she was a different person then — and the two creatures draw close on either side, eyes and mouths opening wider and wider. The account of repeating 'You are old, Father William' to the Caterpillar, with all the words coming different, unsettles them so much that the Mock Turtle demands a fresh experiment: 'Stand up and repeat 'Tis the voice of the sluggard.'' Her head so full of the Lobster Quadrille, Alice can hardly help herself, and the words come very queer indeed: ''Tis the voice of the Lobster; I heard him declare, You have baked me too brown, I must sugar my hair' — and a second verse in which an Owl shares a pie with a Panther, and the banquet concludes badly for the Owl. 'What is the use of repeating all that stuff,' the Mock Turtle interrupts, 'if you don't explain it as you go on? It's by far the most confusing thing I ever heard!'

'Shall we try another figure of the Lobster Quadrille — or would you like the Mock Turtle to sing you a song?' A song, please, if the Mock Turtle would be so kind; and he sighs deeply and begins, in a voice sometimes choked with sobs: 'Beautiful Soup, so rich and green, waiting in a hot tureen! Who for such dainties would not stoop? Soup of the evening, beautiful Soup! ... Soo—oop of the e—e—evening, Beautiful, beautiful Soup!' Midway through the repeated chorus, a cry of 'The trial's beginning!' is heard in the distance. 'Come on!' cries the Gryphon, and, taking Alice by the hand, it hurries off without waiting for the end of the song. 'What trial is it?' Alice pants as she runs; but the Gryphon only answers 'Come on!' and runs the faster, while more and more faintly behind them the breeze carries the melancholy words: 'Soo—oop of the e—e—evening, Beautiful, beautiful Soup!'

segments.heading: XI · Who Stole the Tarts?
segments.text: The King and Queen of Hearts are seated on their throne when Alice and the Gryphon arrive, with a great crowd assembled about them — all sorts of little birds and beasts, as well as the whole pack of cards; the Knave stands before them in chains, a soldier on each side; and on a table in the very middle of the court sits a large dish of tarts, so good-looking that merely seeing them makes Alice quite hungry. Proud of the words she knows from her reading, she identifies the judge — the King himself, wearing his crown over his great wig and looking neither comfortable nor becoming — and the jury-box, where twelve creatures write busily on slates. They are putting down their names, the Gryphon whispers, for fear of forgetting them before the end of the trial. 'Stupid things!' Alice begins, loudly and indignantly, and stops in a hurry at the White Rabbit's cry of 'Silence in the court!'; her revenge falls on the one juror whose pencil squeaks — Bill the Lizard — which she takes from behind so quietly that he must write with one finger for the rest of the day, leaving no mark on the slate. Then the White Rabbit blows three blasts on the trumpet and reads the accusation: 'The Queen of Hearts, she made some tarts, all on a summer day: the Knave of Hearts, he stole those tarts, and took them quite away!'

'Consider your verdict,' says the King, and must be headed off — 'Not yet, not yet! There's a great deal to come before that!' The first witness is the Hatter, who comes in with a teacup in one hand and a piece of bread-and-butter in the other, begging pardon: he hadn't quite finished his tea when he was sent for. When it began is settled among the tea-party as the fourteenth of March ('Fifteenth,' says the March Hare. 'Sixteenth,' adds the Dormouse. 'Write that down,' says the King, and the jury adds up the three dates and reduces the answer to shillings and pence). The hat he wears is not his — 'Stolen!' exclaims the King, turning to the jury — no: he keeps them to sell; he is a hatter; he has none of his own. Meanwhile Alice feels the queerest sensation: she is beginning to grow larger again, seat by seat, to the immediate grievance of the Dormouse squeezed beside her ('You've no right to grow here.' 'Don't talk nonsense — you know you're growing too.' 'Yes, but I grow at a reasonable pace, not in that ridiculous fashion'), which crosses the court sulkily to the other side.

'Give your evidence,' says the King, 'and don't be nervous, or I'll have you executed on the spot.' The Hatter's testimony — 'I'm a poor man, your Majesty' — proceeds by the twinkling of the tea and the flutter of the bread-and-butter ('The twinkling of the what?' 'It began with the tea.' 'Of course twinkling begins with a T! Do you take me for a dunce?'), with the March Hare denying everything and the Dormouse's contribution lost beyond recall ('You must remember, or I'll have you executed'). One guinea-pig cheers and is immediately suppressed by the officers of the court — head-first into a canvas bag, and sat upon — and Alice is pleased to finally understand what the newspapers mean by 'there was some attempt at applause, which was immediately suppressed'; presently the other guinea-pig follows. Invited to stand down, the Hatter explains he can't go no lower, being on the floor as it is; permitted to sit down instead, and at last released — 'You may go' — he bolts for the door without even waiting to put on his shoes, the Queen's instruction ('—and just take his head off outside') arriving after he is out of reach.

The next witness is the Duchess's cook, pepper-box in hand — guessable before she enters, from the people near the door all sneezing at once. 'Give your evidence,' says the King. 'Shan't,' says the cook. The King, coached in a whisper by the anxious White Rabbit ('Your Majesty must cross-examine this witness'), folds his arms, frowns till his eyes are nearly out of sight, and asks in a deep voice: 'What are tarts made of?' 'Pepper, mostly,' says the cook. 'Treacle,' says a sleepy voice behind her — and the Queen goes off like a firework: 'Collar that Dormouse! Behead that Dormouse! Turn that Dormouse out of court! Suppress him! Pinch him! Off with his whiskers!' In the confusion of putting the Dormouse out, the cook disappears entirely. 'Never mind!' says the King, with an air of great relief. 'Call the next witness' — and, the Rabbit fumbling over the list, Alice watches, curious to see who it will be, 'for they haven't got much evidence yet,' she says to herself. Imagine her surprise when the White Rabbit reads out, at the top of his shrill little voice, the name: 'Alice!'

segments.heading: XII · Alice’s Evidence
segments.text: 'Here!' cries Alice — quite forgetting, in the flurry of the moment, how large she has grown in the last few minutes — and jumps up in such a hurry that she tips over the jury-box with the edge of her skirt, upsetting all the jurymen onto the heads of the crowd below, where they sprawl about looking very like the globe of goldfish she accidentally upset the week before. The trial cannot proceed until every juror is back in his proper place — the King is most emphatic on the point — so she shovels them up again as quickly as she can (the poor little Lizard goes in head downward and must be righted), and the slates and pencils resume. Her evidence, once the King asks what she knows about this business, is 'Nothing.' 'Nothing whatever?' 'Nothing whatever.' 'That's very important,' says the King, turning to the jury — 'Unimportant, your Majesty means, of course,' interposes the White Rabbit, very respectfully, but frowning and making faces — 'Unimportant, of course, I meant,' the King hastily says, and goes on to himself in an undertone, 'important—unimportant—unimportant—important—', as if trying which word sounds best. Some of the jury write it down 'important', and some 'unimportant'.

At this moment the King, who has been busily writing in his note-book for some time, calls out 'Silence!' and reads from the book: 'Rule Forty-two. All persons more than a mile high to leave the court.' Everybody looks at Alice. 'I'm not a mile high,' says Alice. 'You are,' says the King. 'Nearly two miles high,' adds the Queen. 'Well, I shan't go, at any rate,' says Alice; 'besides, that's not a regular rule: you invented it just now.' 'It's the oldest rule in the book,' says the King. 'Then it ought to be Number One,' says Alice. The King turns pale and shuts his note-book hastily. 'Consider your verdict,' he says to the jury, in a low, trembling voice.

But there is more evidence to come: a paper just picked up — a letter, perhaps, written by the prisoner to somebody — in fact a set of verses, not in the Knave's handwriting, and not signed. 'He must have imitated somebody else's hand,' says the King; and to the Knave's protest the King returns the day's most applauded logic: 'If you didn't sign it, that only proves your guilt: you must have meant some mischief, or else you'd have signed your name like an honest man.' The verses themselves — 'They told me you had been to her, and mentioned me to him... I gave her one, they gave him two, you gave us three or more' — name nobody, and the King himself allows there may be no meaning in them, 'which saves a world of trouble, you know, as we needn't try to find any'; and yet, examined closely — 'said I could not swim: you can't swim, can you?' (the Knave, being made of cardboard, certainly cannot) — they become, he declares, the most important piece of evidence heard yet. Alice, grown large enough now not to fear interrupting, calls it not a bit of it: 'Why, you don't even know what they're about!' — and the King, peering hopefully at 'It's a pun!', gets no laughter at all.

'Let the jury consider their verdict,' says the King, for about the twentieth time that day. 'No, no!' says the Queen. 'Sentence first — verdict afterwards.' 'Stuff and nonsense!' says Alice loudly — she has grown to her full size by this time — 'the idea of having the sentence first!' 'Off with her head!' the Queen shouts at the top of her voice. Nobody moves. 'Who cares for you?' says Alice. 'You're nothing but a pack of cards!' At this the whole pack rises up into the air and comes flying down upon her: she gives a little scream, half of fright and half of anger, tries to beat them off — and finds herself lying on the bank with her head in the lap of her sister, who is gently brushing away some dead leaves that have fluttered down from the trees upon her face. She tells her curious dream over tea; and her sister, left alone in the setting sun, half-dreams it after her — teacups rattling into sheep-bells, the Queen's shrieks into the shepherd-boy's voice — and pictures how this same little Alice will keep, through all her riper years, the simple and loving heart of her childhood, and gather other little children about her, and make their eyes bright and eager with many a strange tale — perhaps even with the dream of Wonderland of long ago.
